questTitle: Sec 2 Science: Chemical & Physical Change Worksheet 1
subject: Science (Chemistry)
description: Test your understanding of chemical and physical changes! This worksheet covers rusting, combustion, acid reactions, gas tests, electrolysis, electroplating, and thermal decomposition. Read each question carefully and apply your knowledge!

questions.problemSet: CONCEPT CHECK: Chemical vs Physical Change
questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of a CHEMICAL change?
questions.answer: New substance is formed

questions.problemSet: CONCEPT CHECK: Chemical vs Physical Change (continued)
questions.question: Based on what you know about chemical changes (new substance formed), which of the following is a PHYSICAL change?
questions.answer: Melting of ice

questions.problemSet: CONCEPT CHECK: Chemical vs Physical Change (continued)
questions.question: You identified that melting of ice is a physical change because no new substance is formed. Using the same reasoning, why is rusting considered a CHEMICAL change?
options: A new substance (iron oxide) is formed
questions.answer: A new substance (iron oxide) is formed

questions.question: What are the TWO conditions needed for iron to rust?
questions.answer: Water and oxygen

questions.question:
You learned that BOTH water AND oxygen are needed for rusting. A student sets up three test tubes with iron nails:

• Tube A: Iron nail in dry air (calcium chloride absorbs all moisture)
• Tube B: Iron nail in boiled water covered with oil layer
• Tube C: Iron nail in tap water exposed to air

Which tube will show rusting after one week?
questions.answer: Tube C only

questions.question: In the previous experiment, Tube A has dry air (no water) and Tube C has both water and oxygen (so it rusts). For Tube B, the nail is in boiled water covered with oil. Why does this nail NOT rust?
options: Oil reacts with water to prevent rusting
Boiled water is too hot for rusting
Boiled water contains chemicals that prevent rusting
Boiled water has no dissolved oxygen, and the oil layer prevents oxygen from entering
questions.answer: Boiled water has no dissolved oxygen, and the oil layer prevents oxygen from entering

questions.question: Since rusting needs water and oxygen, we can prevent rusting by blocking these from reaching the iron. Which of the following is NOT a correct method to prevent rusting?
options: Applying a layer of oil or grease
questions.answer: Washing with water regularly

questions.question: Painting is a common method to prevent rusting. How exactly does painting prevent iron from rusting?
options: Paint reacts with iron to make it stronger
Paint absorbs all the oxygen in the air
Paint acts as a protective barrier, preventing water and oxygen from reaching the iron
Paint makes the iron surface too smooth for rust to form
questions.answer: Paint acts as a protective barrier, preventing water and oxygen from reaching the iron

questions.question: A bicycle chain is made of iron and is starting to rust. Based on what you learned about rusting conditions (water + oxygen) and prevention methods (blocking water and oxygen), which combination of actions would BEST prevent further rusting?
options: Wash with water and leave to dry in the sun
Clean off existing rust, dry thoroughly, and apply oil
Paint over the rust without cleaning
Soak in salt water to remove rust
questions.answer: Clean off existing rust, dry thoroughly, and apply oil

questions.question: What are the THREE conditions needed for combustion (fire) to occur?
questions.answer: Fuel, oxygen, and heat

questions.question: You identified that fuel, oxygen, and heat are needed for fire. These three conditions form what is commonly called the:
questions.answer: Fire triangle

questions.question: When there is plenty of oxygen, a fuel undergoes COMPLETE combustion. What are the products formed during complete combustion?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide and water

questions.question: Complete combustion produces carbon dioxide and water. Which word equation correctly represents the COMPLETE combustion of methane (natural gas used in cooking)?
questions.answer: Methane + Oxygen → Carbon dioxide + Water

questions.question: When there is not enough oxygen, INCOMPLETE combustion occurs. Instead of carbon dioxide, a different gas is formed. What is this gas and why is it dangerous?
options: Carbon dioxide - it causes global warming
Carbon monoxide - it is a poisonous gas
questions.answer: Carbon monoxide - it is a poisonous gas

questions.question: The fire triangle shows that fire needs fuel, oxygen, and heat. To put out a fire, we need to remove at least ONE of these. A fire breaks out in a kitchen due to burning cooking oil. Which method would be MOST effective and safe?
options: Pour water on it (water can cause hot oil to splatter)
Blow air on it to cool it down
Cover with a fire blanket to cut off oxygen supply
Add more oil to dilute it
questions.answer: Cover with a fire blanket to cut off oxygen supply

questions.question: When a metal reacts with an acid, what are the TWO products formed?
questions.answer: Salt and hydrogen gas

questions.question: You learned that metal + acid produces salt + hydrogen. Which word equation correctly shows the reaction between zinc and hydrochloric acid?
options: Zinc + Hydrochloric acid → Zinc chloride + Water
Zinc + Hydrochloric acid → Zinc chloride + Oxygen
Zinc + Hydrochloric acid → Zinc chloride + Hydrogen
Zinc + Hydrochloric acid → Zinc chloride + Carbon dioxide
questions.answer: Zinc + Hydrochloric acid → Zinc chloride + Hydrogen

questions.question: During the reaction between magnesium ribbon and dilute sulfuric acid, bubbles are observed rising from the magnesium. Based on the equation Metal + Acid → Salt + Hydrogen, what gas is being produced?
questions.answer: Hydrogen

questions.question: When a carbonate compound reacts with an acid, what are the THREE products formed?
questions.answer: Salt, water, and carbon dioxide

questions.question: You learned that carbonate + acid produces salt + water + carbon dioxide. Calcium carbonate (found in limestone, chalk, and seashells) reacts with hydrochloric acid. Which word equation is correct?
options: Calcium carbonate + Hydrochloric acid → Calcium chloride + Water + Hydrogen
Calcium carbonate + Hydrochloric acid → Calcium chloride + Water + Oxygen
Calcium carbonate + Hydrochloric acid → Calcium chloride + Hydrogen + Carbon dioxide
Calcium carbonate + Hydrochloric acid → Calcium chloride + Water + Carbon dioxide
questions.answer: Calcium carbonate + Hydrochloric acid → Calcium chloride + Water + Carbon dioxide

questions.question: When an acid reacts with an alkali (base), what are the TWO products formed?
questions.answer: Salt and water

questions.question: The reaction between an acid and an alkali produces salt and water. This type of reaction has a special name. What is it called?
questions.answer: Neutralisation

questions.question: In a neutralisation reaction, sodium hydroxide (an alkali) reacts with hydrochloric acid. The salt formed takes its first name from the metal in the alkali (sodium) and its second name from the acid (chloride from hydrochloric). What salt is formed?
questions.answer: Sodium chloride

questions.question:
Let's review all three acid reactions:
• Acid + Metal → Salt + Hydrogen
• Acid + Carbonate → Salt + Water + Carbon dioxide
• Acid + Alkali → Salt + Water

A student adds dilute hydrochloric acid to an unknown white powder. Bubbles are produced and the gas turns limewater milky. The unknown substance is most likely a:
options: Carbonate (produces CO₂ which turns limewater milky)
questions.answer: Carbonate (produces CO₂ which turns limewater milky)

questions.question: What is the standard laboratory test to confirm that a gas is carbon dioxide?
options: Insert a lighted splint and listen for a pop sound
See if the gas is colourless
Bubble the gas through limewater and see if it turns milky/cloudy
questions.answer: Bubble the gas through limewater and see if it turns milky/cloudy

questions.question: You learned that carbon dioxide turns limewater milky. This happens because carbon dioxide reacts with limewater to form a new white substance. This white substance is:
options: Calcium carbonate (which is insoluble and appears as white cloudiness)
questions.answer: Calcium carbonate (which is insoluble and appears as white cloudiness)

questions.question: What is the standard laboratory test to confirm that a gas is hydrogen?
options: Bubble through limewater and see if it turns milky
Insert a lighted splint and listen for a 'pop' sound
Smell the gas for a sharp odour
See if the gas rises upward
questions.answer: Insert a lighted splint and listen for a 'pop' sound

questions.question: When testing for hydrogen, the lighted splint makes a 'pop' sound and gets extinguished (goes out). Why does this happen?
options: Hydrogen absorbs all the heat from the flame
Hydrogen is heavier than air and pushes the flame away
Hydrogen burns rapidly with oxygen in a small explosion, using up the available oxygen
questions.answer: Hydrogen burns rapidly with oxygen in a small explosion, using up the available oxygen

questions.question:
A student collects a gas from a chemical reaction in a test tube. She performs two tests:

Test 1: Inserts a lighted splint → Hears a squeaky 'pop' sound and the splint goes out
Test 2: Bubbles another sample through limewater → Limewater remains clear

Based on these results, what gas did she collect?
options: Hydrogen (gives pop sound with lighted splint, doesn't affect limewater)
questions.answer: Hydrogen (gives pop sound with lighted splint, doesn't affect limewater)

questions.question: What is electrolysis?
options: Using electricity to break down a compound into simpler substances
Using heat to break down a compound
Using electricity to join elements together
Using light to break down a compound
questions.answer: Using electricity to break down a compound into simpler substances

questions.question: For electrolysis to work, electric current must be able to flow through the compound. The compound must therefore be:
options: Dissolved in water or in molten (melted) form
questions.answer: Dissolved in water or in molten (melted) form

questions.question: You learned that the compound must be dissolved or molten for electrolysis to work. Why is this necessary?
options: So the charged particles (ions) are free to move and carry the electric current
So it can conduct heat better
So the compound can evaporate easily
questions.answer: So the charged particles (ions) are free to move and carry the electric current

questions.question: During electrolysis of water (with a little acid added to help conduct electricity), gases are produced at both electrodes. What gases are produced?
options: Hydrogen at the negative electrode, oxygen at the positive electrode
questions.answer: Hydrogen at the negative electrode, oxygen at the positive electrode

questions.question:
In the electrolysis of water, a student measures the gases collected:
• Gas at negative electrode: 20 cm³
• Gas at positive electrode: 10 cm³

The volume of hydrogen is TWICE the volume of oxygen. This shows that water contains:
options: Equal amounts of hydrogen and oxygen
Twice as many hydrogen particles as oxygen particles
Twice as many oxygen particles as hydrogen particles
questions.answer: Twice as many hydrogen particles as oxygen particles

questions.question: Electrolysis is used in industry to extract aluminium metal from aluminium ore. Why can't we simply heat aluminium ore with carbon (like we do for iron) to extract the aluminium?
options: Aluminium is too cheap to use carbon
Carbon does not exist in nature
Aluminium is very reactive, so it holds onto oxygen very strongly and cannot be separated by heating with carbon alone
Heating aluminium ore would produce toxic gases
questions.answer: Aluminium is very reactive, so it holds onto oxygen very strongly and cannot be separated by heating with carbon alone

questions.question: What is electroplating?
options: Using electricity to coat an object with a thin layer of metal
Using electricity to break down metals
Using heat to melt metals together
Using chemicals to remove metal coatings
questions.answer: Using electricity to coat an object with a thin layer of metal

questions.question: In electroplating, we have two electrodes connected to a power supply. The object we want to coat with metal is connected to which electrode?
options: Neither electrode - it floats in the solution
questions.answer: The negative electrode

questions.question: You learned that the object to be plated is connected to the negative electrode. The metal that will be used as the coating is connected to which electrode?
questions.answer: The positive electrode

questions.question: Electroplating is used for many purposes. Which of the following is NOT a reason for electroplating an object?
options: To make the object look more attractive (e.g., gold-plated jewellery)
To protect the object from rusting or corrosion
To make the object more durable and scratch-resistant
To make the object lighter in weight
questions.answer: To make the object lighter in weight

questions.question:
A factory wants to electroplate steel spoons with silver to make them look more attractive. Based on what you learned:
• Object to be plated → negative electrode
• Coating metal → positive electrode
• Solution must contain ions of the coating metal

Which setup is correct?
options: Steel spoon at positive electrode, silver at negative electrode, in silver nitrate solution
Steel spoon at negative electrode, silver at positive electrode, in silver nitrate solution
Steel spoon at negative electrode, silver at positive electrode, in sodium chloride solution
Steel spoon at positive electrode, silver at negative electrode, in sodium chloride solution
questions.answer: Steel spoon at negative electrode, silver at positive electrode, in silver nitrate solution

questions.question: What is thermal decomposition?
options: Breaking down a compound using electricity
Breaking down a compound using heat
Dissolving a compound in hot water
questions.answer: Breaking down a compound using heat

questions.question: Thermal decomposition uses heat to break down compounds. When copper carbonate (a green powder) is heated strongly, it decomposes into:
questions.answer: Copper oxide (black) and carbon dioxide

questions.question:
You learned that heating copper carbonate produces copper oxide and carbon dioxide. A student heats green copper carbonate powder and observes:
• The green powder turns black
• A gas is released

How can the student confirm that the gas released is carbon dioxide?
options: Bubble the gas through limewater and check if it turns milky
Insert a lighted splint and listen for a pop sound
See if the gas is visible
questions.answer: Bubble the gas through limewater and check if it turns milky

questions.question: Most metal carbonates decompose when heated. However, some carbonates of very reactive metals (like sodium and potassium) do NOT decompose easily with a Bunsen burner. Which of the following carbonates does NOT decompose when heated with a Bunsen burner?
options: Sodium carbonate (too stable, does not decompose with Bunsen burner)
questions.answer: Sodium carbonate (too stable, does not decompose with Bunsen burner)

questions.question:
In the construction industry, quicklime (calcium oxide) is produced by heating limestone (calcium carbonate) in large kilns. This is thermal decomposition:

Calcium carbonate → Calcium oxide + ?

What is the other product of this reaction?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide

questions.question:
A student adds dilute sulfuric acid to magnesium ribbon. The magnesium ribbon fizzes rapidly and eventually disappears. Bubbles of gas are produced.

Recall: Metal + Acid → Salt + Hydrogen

What type of reaction is this?
questions.answer: Acid + Metal reaction

questions.question: In the previous question, magnesium reacted with sulfuric acid to produce a salt and hydrogen gas. The hydrogen gas produced is tested with a lighted splint. What would you expect to observe?
options: The splint continues to burn normally
The splint burns with a squeaky 'pop' sound and goes out
questions.answer: The splint burns with a squeaky 'pop' sound and goes out

questions.question:
Sodium hydroxide solution (an alkali) is added to dilute nitric acid. The mixture becomes warm, showing that a reaction has occurred.

Recall: Acid + Alkali → Salt + Water

What is the name of this type of reaction?
questions.answer: Neutralisation

questions.question:
Comparing rusting and electrolysis:

• Rusting needs water and oxygen
• Electrolysis needs electricity and a dissolved/molten compound

Both are examples of chemical changes because:
options: Both involve a change in temperature
Both involve a change in colour only
Both produce new substances that are different from the starting materials
questions.answer: Both produce new substances that are different from the starting materials

questions.question:
A student performs four experiments:

Experiment 1: Heats green copper carbonate powder → Gas produced turns limewater milky
Experiment 2: Adds hydrochloric acid to sodium carbonate → Fizzing occurs, gas turns limewater milky
Experiment 3: Adds hydrochloric acid to zinc metal → Fizzing occurs, gas gives 'pop' sound with lighted splint
Experiment 4: Electrolyses water → Gas at negative electrode gives 'pop' sound with lighted splint

Which experiments produce HYDROGEN gas?
options: Experiments 3 and 4 (both produce hydrogen - pop sound test)
questions.answer: Experiments 3 and 4 (both produce hydrogen - pop sound test)
